critique.summary: The article introduces GreenBoost, an open-source Linux kernel module and CUDA userspace shim that extends NVIDIA GPU VRAM using system RAM and NVMe storage to run large language models exceeding native GPU memory. It transparently manages memory tiers without modifying official NVIDIA drivers, aiming to improve performance over CPU offloading or buying larger GPUs.
mainPoints: GreenBoost extends GPU VRAM by using system DDR4 RAM and NVMe storage via a kernel module and CUDA shim without altering NVIDIA drivers.
It targets large language model inference workloads that exceed GPU memory, providing a three-tier memory system: GPU VRAM, DDR4 RAM, and NVMe swap.
The solution uses DMA-BUF and CUDA external memory import features to allow GPU direct access to system memory over PCIe 4.0.
GreenBoost bundles optimized inference libraries and tools for quantization, compression, and fine-tuning to maximize performance.
Installation is automated with hardware detection and system tuning scripts, supporting Ubuntu and Red Hat-based Linux distributions.
Known limitations include dependency on recent Linux kernels, specific handling for certain inference engines, and NVMe latency constraints.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with known Linux kernel and CUDA features such as DMA-BUF and external memory imports, which are documented NVIDIA capabilities. The described approach aligns with current research and development trends in GPU memory extension for large AI models. The repository and author information appear legitimate, and the technical details match publicly available CUDA and Linux kernel documentation. However, the article is a project README from a GitLab repository, not a journalistic source, and lacks independent third-party verification or benchmarking beyond the author's tests. The performance claims and compatibility notes are plausible but should be independently validated by users or reviewers.
considerations: Performance impact of using system RAM and NVMe as extended GPU memory depends heavily on PCIe bandwidth and latency, which may limit real-time inference speed.
Compatibility with different GPUs, drivers, and inference frameworks may vary and require additional adaptation or development.
Security and stability implications of loading a third-party kernel module alongside NVIDIA drivers should be carefully evaluated.
The approach does not replace NVIDIA drivers or virtualize GPUs, so it may not support all GPU features or workloads.
Users should consider the trade-offs between model quantization, offloading strategies, and hardware upgrades when choosing memory extension solutions.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Nvidia GreenBoost, a project to extend GPU VRAM using system RAM and NVMe. Commenters debate its practicality, performance compared to existing solutions, and potential limitations such as speed and code quality.

comments.author: 0xbadcafebee
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed technical explanation of existing methods and highlights the impracticality of using system RAM for large models due to slow memory bandwidth, making it highly accurate.

comments.author: nl
comments.text:
This is really interesting engineering, but I agree with the other commentators that the benchmarking makes it hard to understand the contribution various factors are having.

The ExLlamaV3 EXL3 2bpw (8 GB, full VRAM) row is an order of magnitude faster than the baseline - but the baseline seems to be the 32GB model running with the KV cache shared to system memory only (I think?)

But if a 8GB model gives sufficient quality then it seems like that would have worked without the shared memory thing?

I think the useful apples-to-apples benchmark is currently the Ollama + GreenBoost shim (baseline) (2-5 tps) vs ExLlamaV3 + GreenBoost cache (8–20 tps) comparison.

It would be really useful to see this compared with the existing llama CPU/memory offload. There is a note at the start ("Offload layers to CPU — works, but drops token/s by 5–10× because CPU RAM has no CUDA coherence") - but it is unclear if that 5-10x token speed drop is compared to running a model completely in GPU or compared to the greenboost approach.

I think it is vs GPU, in which case it seems likely the performance is similar to what greenboost is giving but probably much more stable.
comments.analysis: The comment thoughtfully critiques the benchmarking methodology and suggests more meaningful comparisons, showing a good understanding of performance evaluation.

comments.author: ninjagoo
comments.text:
This is awesome! Normally, offloading layers to the CPU RAM means that the compute for those layers occurs on the CPU instead of the GPU, generally speaking. The CPU is orders of magnitude slower than the GPU.

With this approach the compute occurs on the GPU, with the tradeoff that layers in RAM have to be moved back-and-forth through PCI-DMA. It seems to me that this should offer a speedup vs compute split between GPU and CPU. The amount of speedup will depend on how many layers would have been on CPU compute, minus the reduction due to moving those layers between RAM and the GPU.

What's slower? Compute on the CPU or moving data from RAM to GPU through PCI-DMA?
comments.analysis: This comment accurately explains the technical tradeoffs between CPU and GPU compute and data transfer overhead, providing a nuanced view of the approach's potential benefits.

comments.author: ma2kx
comments.text:
The physical bottleneck to system memory remains. Therefore, I assume that better results are achieved by manually adjusting which layers are offloaded.

I would prefer to use system memory to cache different models, focusing on things like embedding, rerankers, and TTS. This is sufficient to run a more complex RAG locally, for example, via Mem0, and then use a larger LLM via the cloud.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies system memory bandwidth as a bottleneck and suggests practical usage scenarios, showing a realistic understanding of the technology.

comments.author: 152334H
comments.text:
Nobody mentioning how this project is vibecoded slop?

  > The code is really bad with completely uneeded parts. The LLM (Qwen 2.5 7B) has hardcoded the i9 14700KF topology, and has variables related to it never used... It's even funnier that the show hardware function always prints the same string. There are even random pip log files. Why did this slop got coverage here?
https://www.phoronix.com/forums/forum/linux-graphics-x-org-d...
comments.analysis: While the comment raises valid concerns about code quality, its tone is emotional and dismissive, reducing its constructive value despite likely accuracy.

comments.author: paultendo
comments.text: Could be a very useful way to do some overnight tasks using spare RAM. Possibly things like LLM-based categorisation, labelling, data cleansing. That's what comes to mind for me anyway.
comments.analysis: This comment constructively suggests practical use cases for the technology, showing a positive and realistic perspective.

comments.author: armada651
comments.text: Doesn't Windows already do this by default? I can already run models bigger than my GPU VRAM and it will start using up to 50% of my system RAM as "shared memory". This is on a Desktop PC without a shared memory architecture.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly notes that Windows uses shared system RAM for GPU memory overflow, providing relevant context though not deeply technical.

comments.author: holoduke
comments.text: The is extremely slow and not useful in my opinion.
comments.analysis: While brief and subjective, this comment aligns with other remarks about speed limitations, though it lacks detailed justification.

comments.author: bguberfain
comments.text: "A watchdog kernel thread monitors RAM and NVMe pressure and signals userspace before things get dangerous." - which kind of danger this type of solution can have?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a relevant question about system stability and potential risks, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: Berazu
comments.text: I wish there was a way to extend RAM/NVMe with GPU VRAM. :(
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a wishful idea but lacks technical insight or constructive contribution.
